coredata.dc:title: A fluorescence switch-off nanosensor for sensitive determination of the antiandrogen drug flutamide in pharmaceutical and environmental samples. Analytical method greenness, blueness, and whiteness assessment

coredata.dc:description: A fast, practical, and non-hazardous switch-off fluorescent nanosensor for the non-fluorescent antiandrogen drug flutamide (FLU) was developed from nitrogen/sulfur co-doping of carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs). For the first time, the highly fluorescent N,S-CQDs were generated via one-pot microwave-assisted synthesis in only 3 min using facilely available precursors (sweet yellow pepper and thiourea). The sensor is characterized by a narrow particle size distribution, high doping efficiency (N, 42.05 % and S, 8.28 %), reproducibility, and high emission at 410 nm after excitation at 330 nm. FLU efficiently and quantitatively turned off the fluorescence of the prepared N,S-CQDs via a synergistic combination of static quenching and inner filter effect. The furnished nanosensor showed good linearity (r = 0.9999) for FLU analysis within concentrations ranged from 1.0 to 30.0 µg mL−1 with a detection limit of 0.293 µg mL−1 and quantitation limit of 0.886 µg mL−1. The selectivity of the probe was confirmed in the presence of co-administered drugs, possible co-existing materials, and various metal ions. The N,S-CQDs interestingly showed acceptable cytocompatibility and low toxicity, as revealed by the MTT assay. This feature bestows the N,S-CQDs a practicability as an environmental sensor. Thus, the proposed approach was implemented for FLU analysis in pharmaceutical tablets, tap water, and river water with good percentage recoveries (99.59 ± 1.28%, 99.49 ± 1.75%, and 101.21 ± 1.67%, respectively) and without interferences. Furthermore, the approach was positively assessed with respect to greenness, blueness, and whiteness. The high values of the analytical greenness score AGREE (0.78), the chiefly green ComplexGAPI pictogram, the Blue Applicability Grade Index BAGI (72.5), and the RGB12 (93.6) show the high greenness, blueness, and whiteness features of the method that confirm its minor environmental impact, excellent applicability, and sustainability, respectively.

head.ce:title: A fluorescence switch-off nanosensor for sensitive determination of the antiandrogen drug flutamide in pharmaceutical and environmental samples. Analytical method greenness, blueness, and whiteness assessment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fast, practical, and non-hazardous switch-off fluorescent nanosensor for the non-fluorescent antiandrogen drug flutamide (FLU) was developed from nitrogen/sulfur co-doping of carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs). For the first time, the highly fluorescent N,S-CQDs were generated via one-pot microwave-assisted synthesis in only 3 min using facilely available precursors (sweet yellow pepper and thiourea). The sensor is characterized by a narrow particle size distribution, high doping efficiency (N, 42.05 % and S, 8.28 %), reproducibility, and high emission at 410 nm after excitation at 330 nm. FLU efficiently and quantitatively turned off the fluorescence of the prepared N,S-CQDs via a synergistic combination of static quenching and inner filter effect. The furnished nanosensor showed good linearity (r = 0.9999) for FLU analysis within concentrations ranged from 1.0 to 30.0 µg mL−1 with a detection limit of 0.293 µg mL−1 and quantitation limit of 0.886 µg mL−1. The selectivity of the probe was confirmed in the presence of co-administered drugs, possible co-existing materials, and various metal ions. The N,S-CQDs interestingly showed acceptable cytocompatibility and low toxicity, as revealed by the MTT assay. This feature bestows the N,S-CQDs a practicability as an environmental sensor. Thus, the proposed approach was implemented for FLU analysis in pharmaceutical tablets, tap water, and river water with good percentage recoveries (99.59 ± 1.28%, 99.49 ± 1.75%, and 101.21 ± 1.67%, respectively) and without interferences. Furthermore, the approach was positively assessed with respect to greenness, blueness, and whiteness. The high values of the analytical greenness score AGREE (0.78), the chiefly green ComplexGAPI pictogram, the Blue Applicability Grade Index BAGI (72.5), and the RGB12 (93.6) show the high greenness, blueness, and whiteness features of the method that confirm its minor environmental impact, excellent applicability, and sustainability, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Prostate cancer ranks as the second most prevalent cancer among men and the fifth greatest cause of mortality globally [1]. According to World Health Organization data, 1,414,259 new prostate cancer cases and 375,304 new deaths are registered annually, and the mortality rate from prostate cancer is expected to double in the next twenty years, reaching 2.3 million cases [2]. Testosterone is the most essential component behind boosting the expansion and proliferation of prostate cancer cells. One of the primary groups of medications for treating prostate cancer are cytostatic medications. Cytostatic agents are a broad category of carcinogenic, mutagenic, and teratogenic medications used in the treatment of cancer, infections, and skin conditions. Among them, flutamide (FLU), methyl-N-[nitro-3-(trifluoromethyl) phenyl]-propanamide [3], is a non-steroidal antiandrogen agent commonly used in clinical settings for advanced prostate cancer patients. Recently, FLU, as an adjunctive drug, showed promising results for reducing COVID-19 severity and the infection with severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 in prostate cancer patients [4]. It inhibits the binding of testosterone to the intracellular androgen receptors within target tissues like prostate, testis, hair follicles, and skin [5]. As a result, it affects the transcription of transmembrane protease serine 2 gene, which is necessary for viral entry and infection [4]. FLU also reduces C19 steroids metabolism via the cytochrome P450 system in the target-specific cells of the secondary sex organs [6]. Comparably, it can be employed as a treatment for high androgen levels in females, particularly those suffering from polycystic ovary syndrome. From this vantage point, diminishing fertility rates have arisen as a major social worry, and thus overuse of FLU and related anticancer medications has drawn public and scientific interest in recent times. The overdosage of FLU can cause serious adverse effects such as an inflamed prostate, vomiting, diarrhea, blood in the urine, nausea, loss of sexual interest, hot flashes, sleepiness, severe hepatotoxicity, swelling of male breasts, and methemoglobinemia [7].

Furthermore, FLU may be released into the environment through feces discharge and urine as an unchanged medicine. Thus, it is considered as an emerging micropollutant that falls within the category of endocrine disrupting compounds. FLU show good stability at neutral pH and room temperature. Besides, it is poorly biodegradable. Thus it is expected to remain unchanged upon release to environmental water [8]. The exposure to this contaminant in aquatic habitat alters fish gonad growth and function as well as the reproductive process [9]. Its probable hazard as a water pollutant presents a serious problem to the ecosystem and aquatic life, affecting the food chain and necessitating frequent monitoring. Thus, given its significance, it is imperative to develop a sensitive analytical technique with high selectivity for the monitoring of this cytostatic agent in medicaments and environmental water. In this context, the analytical literature has cited different procedures for FLU determination; most of them are high performance liquid chromatography [10–14], flow injection analysis [15], mass spectrometry [16] and electrochemical analysis [17–19]. Few spectrophotometry [20] and spectrofluorimetry [21,22] have been reported for FLU determination. However, these procedures have several drawbacks, including the lack of selectivity and sensitivity, time-consuming sample preparation, and needing of specific reagents, which limit their applicability to detect FLU in pharmaceuticals and environmental samples. Compared to other techniques, spectrofluorimetric methods proved to be simple, sensitive, time-saving, and widely available at a relatively cheap cost for drug estimation in various matrices. The previously documented spectrofluorimetric procedures for FLU require either reduction with Zn dust/concentrated HCl for 30 min followed by neutralization with NaHCO3 [21] or synthesis of copper nanoclusters using hydrazine, pepsin, and 50 ○C heating for 2 h [22]. As a result, the development of a spectrofluorimetric approach capable of delivering sensitive determination of trace levels of FLU in various matrices in a simple procedure while reducing analytical time will have broad utility.

In the newfangled era of nanotechnology, the conversion of agricultural products into new nanomaterials for a variety of purposes is a successful strategy from the viewpoint of green analytical chemistry. Carbon-based nanomaterials play an important function in the advancement of material science. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) consist of sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon atoms with a diameter ≤10 nm [23]. They have attractive features such as multicolor emission, photobleaching resistance, outstanding biocompatibility, and high solubility in organic and aqueous solvents [24]. Researchers are intrigued by CQDs, a modern rising star within the carbon family, because of their exceptional and controllable photoluminescence, high quantum yield (Ф), small size, minimal cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and low cost. CQDs have emerged as important tools in various fields, including sensors, catalysis, bioimaging, biomedical research, and optoelectronic devices [25,26]. Some of the processes utilized to create CQDs include laser ablation, chemical oxidation, arc discharge, hydrothermal, electrochemical, solvothermal, ultrasonic, and microwave treatment [25]. The most quick, simple, affordable, and environment-friendly of these is microwave-assisted synthesis. Additionally, selective and uniform diffusion of microwave radiation within the sample offers great output, minimal defect, and size control, as well as enhanced safety, greater reproducibility of the results, and exceptional control of experimental conditions for CQDs production [27]. The main merit of employing microwave heating is the contactless heat conveyed to the reactants, allowing the reactions to occur in an unexpectedly brief time [28].

Because of their rising availability and environmental concerns, plants and another green biomass have been used as precursors for CQDs synthesis. In this study, sweet yellow pepper (Capsicum annum L.) was utilized as a promising carbon precursor for the synthesis of CQDs. Bell pepper is one of the world’s most economically and agriculturally significant vegetable crops, with a production volume of 1.04 billion pounds in the USA in 2022 [29]. Yellow peppers contain the highest vitamin C content of all sweet peppers [30]. In addition, they are rich in proteins, potassium, calcium, folic acid, and various antioxidants, such as capsanthin, violaxanthin, lutein, luteolin, and quercetin. These facts imply the possible nitrogen self-doping of the CQDs. Thiourea was added to the yellow pepper juice for additional nitrogen and sulfur doping of the CQDs (N,S-CQDs), showing a strong fluorescence that allowed their applicability as a very effective fluorescence sensor for medical and environmental analysis [31]. Doping CQDs with heteroatoms such as nitrogen and sulfur, enhances their fluorescent properties and photoluminescence quantum yield, which in turn improves the limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantitation (LOQ) and, accordingly, their sensing performance [32,33]. Nitrogen atoms possess similar atomic radius to carbon and can inject electrons directly into the dots, changing their interior electronic environment. Sulfur and carbon atoms have comparable electronegativity, so N,S-CQDs are one of the most important doped CQDs [27,34,35].

In the current study, two tools were employed to assess the greenness profile, namely Complementary green analytical procedure index (ComplexGAPI) [36] and Analytical Greenness Calculator (AGREE) [37]. An important supplement to green analytical chemistry is white analytical chemistry, which considers environmentally friendly factors as well as other important factors that significantly influence the analytical method's quality. Additionally, the recent concepts of blueness were employed, which were in line with sustainable environmental principles [38].

Accordingly, a facile, green, white, blue, selective, and low-cost microwave-assisted procedure was established for the synthesis of N,S-CQDs by using yellow pepper as the precursor for the first time without any hazardous agents or surface passivation chemicals. The N,S-CQDs worked as a switch-off fluorescent probe for the assay of FLU in pharmaceutical tablets and different environmental water samples.

FLU with a purity of 98.82 % was kindly supplied by Schering-Plough, Cairo, Egypt. HPLC grade methanol was supplied by Sigma Aldrich, Germany. Thiourea was bought from Techno Pharmchem, Cairo, Egypt. Starch, cellulose, lactose, sodium lauryl sulfate, colloidal silica, magnesium stearate, the three acids (boric, acetic, and phosphoric), and sodium hydroxide were all bought from the Cairo, Egypt-based company, El Nasr Chemical Co. Human lung fibroblast (WI38) cell line was obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC) via VACSERA, a Cairo-based holding company for biological products and vaccines. Distilled water was used throughout the experiment. A 0.45 µm membrane filter (Millipore, Ireland) was utilized. Tap water was collected from our laboratory in the Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura, Egypt. Nile River water was collected in glass bottles in Mansoura City.

A Cary Eclipse spectrofluorometer is supplied with grating monochromators (5 nm), a xenon lamp, and one cm quartz cell. The voltage was tuned at high mode (800 v). For UV/Vis spectroscopy, Shimadzu type UV/Vis spectrophotometer of Model (1601) (Japan), was used. A Sonix IV (SS101H) 230 (USA), a Consort NV P901 pH meter, and a water bath (Shaker, England) were utilized in the work. Heating was achieved by a WANSA microwave (WP800P28-2, 800 W, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of buffer and standard solution
ce:section.ce:para: Britton-Robinson buffer (BRB) was produced by mixing boric, phosphoric, and acetic acids solution (0.04 M of each) with appropriate volumes of 0.2 M sodium hydroxide to modify the pH (2.0–12.0).

FLU was prepared at a concentration of 100 µg mL−1 in methanol in a 100-mL volumetric flask. The stock solution was kept in the refrigerator after being wrapped in aluminum foil [3], where it was stable for one week. Working solutions of the studied concentration range were prepared by dilution with water.

Fresh sweet yellow peppers were bought from the local Egyptian market and cleaned with tap water followed by distilled water. The peppers were first chopped into tiny pieces before being finely grinded in the household fruit blender. Juice devoid of pulp was obtained by filtering the resultant blend through muslin fabric. Then, thiourea (3 g) was entirely dissolved within 20 mL of pepper juice in a small beaker, and the mixture was placed in the domestic microwave oven for 3 min to get a charred residue. After cooling, 20 mL of distilled water were added, and the mixture was sonicated for 30 min, followed by purification step via centrifugation for 20 min at 6000 rpm and supernatant filtration with a syringe membrane filter (0.22 µm). Five mL of the resultant brown solution were transferred to a 100 mL flask before being filled with water to the mark. The N,S-CQDs stock solution was stored in the refrigerator after being wrapped in aluminum foil and its stability was found to be up to 6 months.

JEM-2100 TEM, JEOL, Japan was used for TEM imaging. FTIR measurement was performed with the help of a Thermo Fisher Scientific FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet − iS20, USA). EDX analysis was achieved with EDX software linked to a JEOL (JSM-6510LV) field emission electron microscope (Japan). The surface chemical composition was further characterized by XPS (K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, Thermo Fisher Scientific, WI, USA). The nanoparticles’ zeta potential was determined by Malvern Zetasizer zeta potential Ver. 2.3., Ltd, UK. Optical characteristics comprising fluorescence and absorbance were likewise examined.

The Ф was examined based on the reference standard, which was quinine sulfate (Ф = 54 % in 0.1 M H2SO4) at an excitation of 310 nm. The synthesized N,S-CQDs Ф was measured with the aid of Eq. (1) [39]:
(1)
Ф x
=
Ф s
×
A s
A x
×
F x
F s
×
η x 2
η s 2
where, F is the integral of the emission scan, A denotes the absorbance, x denotes CQDs, and s is the standard quinine sulfate.

Cell viability of N,S-CQDs was performed by MTT (3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl) − 2,5-diphenyl −2-H-tetrazolium bromide) colorimetric assay. The seeding of WI38 was implemented in 96-well plates, suspended at 1 x 104 cells per well, and cultured in RPMI-1640 medium with 5 mL fetal bovine serum 10 %, 100 units mL−1 penicillin, and 100 µg mL−1 streptomycin. After overnight incubation at 37 ◦C under 5 % CO2, the cells were treated with different N,S-CQDs concentrations. After incubation for 48 h, the cells were treated with an aliquot of MTT solution (5 mg mL−1) in phosphate buffered solution and the plates were incubated for further 4 h at 37 ◦C. DMSO (100 µL) was introduced into each well to solvate the intracellular formazan crystals that had formed. The absorbance at 570 nm was scanned utilizing an EXL 800 BioTek plate reader (USA). The percentage of cell viability was estimated as the absorbance ratio of the treated sample cells with CQDs to the control cells, followed by multiplication by 100 [40,41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for FLU determination by the N,S-CQDs

ce:section.ce:para: Aliquots of 170 µL of N,S-CQDs were added to a set of 10 mL volumetric flasks, followed by adding appropriate volumes of FLU standard solution. The final volume was adjusted using water, and the flasks were thoroughly mixed. The fluorescence intensities (FI) were recorded at λem 398 nm with λex at 310 nm. A blank experiment was prepared similarly to determine its fluorescence intensity (F○), omitting the drug. Calibration graph was done by plotting Ln (F○/F) against drug concentration (µg mL−1) and the linear regression equation was then concluded.

Due to the unavailability of the commercial dosage form in the local (Egyptian) market, laboratory prepared preparation was used in the study. It was used to estimate the active constituent in the presence of pharmaceutical excipients and additives. FLU prepared tablets with a concentration of 250 mg/tablet were prepared using maize starch, cellulose, lactose, colloidal silica, sodium lauryl sulfate, and magnesium stearate [42]. An accurately weighed amount of the powder corresponding to 10.0 mg FLU was transferred to 100 mL calibrated flask after mixing. Thereafter, the drug was extracted with 80 mL of methanol in the ultrasonic bath for 30 min. After sonication, the final volume was completed with methanol, and the solution was filtered to remove insoluble excipients. The general procedure under 2.8.1 was performed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of FLU in tap and environmental waters
ce:section.ce:para: Nile River water was filtered utilizing 0.45 µm membrane filters. Different aliquots of FLU standard stock solution within the linearity range (1.0–30.0 µg mL−1) were spiked to river water and tap water samples, and the procedure was completed as described in 2.8.1.

From the produced FI in all samples, the recoveries of the analyte were obtained from the calibration curve constructed previously or the resulted regression equation, and the standard deviation values were also listed.

The water-soluble N,S-CQDs were synthesized in a short time (simply 3 min) employing green single-pace microwave-driven pyrolysis in a household microwave. The highly fluorescent N,S-CQDs were generated utilizing starting materials readily available all over the world and a straightforward procedure, with yellow pepper as a good source of carbon and thiourea as a nitrogen/sulfur dopant. Yellow pepper has a high glucose content, which gives it a sweet taste. Carotenoids such as α, β carotenes, capsanthin, β-cryptoxanthin, violaxanthin, and lutein are responsible for its brilliant yellow color [43]. Additionally, it is an important source of vitamins (C, B6, A, E, and K1), folic acid, polyphenols, and flavonoids (luteolin and quercetin) [43]. These facts suggest the potential of yellow pepper as a carbon source rich in doping atoms, e.g., nitrogen.

Primarily, the impact of the ratio of yellow pepper juice: thiourea was investigated while maintaining the emitting microwaves period and the ultimate volume of the beginning solution at 20 mL. The ratio of 20 mL: 3 g (pepper juice: thiourea) yielded the highest Ф (Fig. 1
A). This ratio was utilized for studying the microwave irradiation time effect. Exposing the substrates mixture to different microwave irradiation times has been investigated from 2 to 7 min. Three minutes was selected as the optimum irradiation time that gave the highest quantum yield (Fig. 1 B).

To ensure the repeatability and reliability of the synthesis technique, sweet yellow pepper was collected in different months: April, May, July, August, and September, and utilized as the starting material. Also, it was obtained from diverse geographical regions in Egypt; among them are Cairo, Mansoura, and Assiut. The calculated relative standard deviation (%RSD) values were small for N,S-CQDs synthesized individually over several months and from various cultivation areas (2.42 and 0.44 %, respectively). The results of this study are observed in Fig. 1 C and 1D. These values prove the robustness and reproducibility of the established synthesis process and its applicability in different labs across various areas using materials that are readily available. Fig. 2
A depicts a schematic representation of the whole N,S-CQDs synthesis procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent probe characterization and optical properties
ce:section.ce:para:
Various methodologies were used for the descriptive elucidation of the as-prepared carbon dots. TEM imaging showed spherically and uniformly dispersed small N,S-CQDs (Fig. 3
A) with average particle size of 7.3 ± 0.7 nm. FTIR spectroscopy was further conducted to identify the surface functional groups present in the dots. As presented in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 3 B), the transmission peak at 3171 cm−1 corresponds to N-H or O-H groups. The band at 2800 cm−1 reflects the C-H. While the absorption band at 2051 cm−1 referred to −NCS group, the bands at 1740 and 1611 cm−1 correspond to C = O and C = C or C = N groups, respectively. The absorption bands at 1402 and 1200 cm−1 are attributed to COO– and C = S groups, respectively, and the band at 1093 cm−1 could be the result of S = O stretching. The absorption peak at 480 cm−1 is ascribed to S-S stretching [44]. These results demonstrated the presence of O, N, and S functional units at the dots’ surface, resulting in improved fluorescence, electrical behavior, hydrophilicity, and water solubility [45,46]. EDX was also conducted to analyze the N,S-CQDs’ aggregation and elemental composition. SEM image of N,S-CQDs showed nanometrically aggregates of particles ( Fig. 3 C) that probably resulted from the tiny particle size of the N,S-CQDs. The elemental makeup of the prepared CQDs was proven from the spectral analysis of EDX showing a high nitrogen and sulfur doping level along with carbon and oxygen ( Fig. 3 D).

XPS survey scan further displays that there are C, O, N, and S elements on the N,S-CQDs’ surface. In Fig. 3 E, C1s (285 ev), O1s (531 ev), N1s (400 ev), and S2p (163 ev) prove the successful doping of CQDs with N and S. The C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S1 A) was decomposed into four signals at 284.50, 284.76, 288.18, and 286.48 eV, corresponding to C-C/C = C, C-C/C-H, O-C = O/N-C = O, and C-O/C-S/C-N/-N = C = S, respectively [47,48]. The N1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S1 B) exhibits two signals at 398.14 and 400.02 eV, originating from −N = C = S and C-NH2/N in aromatic ring, respectively [49]. The O1s scan spectrum (Fig. S1 C) has three peaks at 531.11, 532.25, and 535.40 eV, corresponding to C = O, C-OH, and O-H, respectively. The S2p spectrum (Fig. S1 D) was attributed to 162.40, 163.62, 165.26, and 168.57, that correspond to −N = C = S, S-S, thiophene, and S = O, respectively [47,49].

Moreover, the zeta potential analysis was performed to evaluate the surface charges, binding affinity as well as the stability of colloidal system. The zeta potential (ζ) value was −25.0 mV (Fig. 3 F), suggesting the existence of negatively charged –COOH and –OH functional groups on the dots’ surface. This negative value increases the electrostatic repulsion between the particles and therefore confirms the good colloidal stability of the carbon dots. Fig. 4
A displays the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of N,S-CQDs, which reveals absorption peaks at 263 and 320 nm. The absorption peak at 263 nm is due to π- π* energy transition of C = C of the aromatic core of the carbon dots, whilst the peak around 320 nm is due to the surface state’s trapping of the excited state energy and n–π* of the C–O and C–N bonds [50,51]. So, strong emission of the dots was produced.

To explore the excitation-dependent emission property of the N,S-CQDs, we scanned the sample from 300 to 400 nm excitation wavelengths (with 10 nm intervals) and collected their emission response (Fig. 4 B). It was observed that the maximum λem gradually shifted to longer λ upon increasing λex, with an increase in FI up to λex 340 nm. Further increase in λex is associated with red shift of λem with a gradual decrease of FI. At an excitation of 330 nm, the maximum fluorescence emission was observed at 410 nm (quantum yield of 9.55 %). The obtained findings were characteristic for CQDs and could be explained by both the diverse surface functional groups on the N,S-CQDs and the quantum confinement effect, which is linked to the particles’ variable sizes [52,53].

It is essential to figure out the cytocompatibility of the N,S-CQDs to ensure their safety, especially before applying to environmental water. The cytotoxicity was studied with the WI38 cell line using the MTT test. Fig. 4 C shows that the viability of WI38 cells was good after incubation with the N,S-CQDs for 48 h. The MTT test outcomes demonstrated that the N,S-CQDs are characterized by good compatibility and low toxicity, with IC50 of 36 µg mL−1 in comparison to IC50 of doxorubicin as a control of 6.72 µg mL−1. Accordingly, the as-prepared probe was well suited for environmental water containing living organisms.

The stability of the as-obtained N,S-CQDs is a fundamental factor for their effective applications. Changing the pH over the range of 2–12 utilizing BRB was attempted to study its influence on the FI of the nanosensor. The FI of CQDs has been decreased by about 20 % only at pH less than 3 or more than 8 (Fig. 4 D). The pH-responsive behavior is ascribed to the surface groups’ protonation and deprotonation, which can alter the energy level of the CQDs [54]. In a strongly low pH medium, protonation of carboxylic and amino groups masks the negative charge, and this hinders the repulsion between the particles. Accordingly, aggregation of the CQDs and diminished stability led to FI quenching [55]. Under alkaline conditions, fluorescence is quenched since CQDs could aggregate due to the development of intermolecular hydrogen bonds [56]. The CQDs demonstrated good stability in aqueous solution throughout a wide pH range of 3.0–8.0, indicating that they have a wide variety of applications. The stability of CQDs at physiological pH is an additional advantage, as they can fit in the biomedical area.

For studying photostability, CQDs solution was exposed to continuous UV-radiation at 365 nm for 30 and 60 min, and the FI diminished by only 10 % after 60 min. The FI of an aqueous solution of CQDs kept at 4 ◦C was measured after 6 months to determine its long-term stability. It was stable, as the percent decrease in FI did not exceed 1 %. Interestingly, CQDs preserved their fluorescence property without any sign of turbidity, so their colloidal stability is good and can be utilized over a long period of time.

The estimation of FLU in this work relies on switching off the fluorescence of the as-prepared N,S-CQDs. Modulation of λex was performed for FLU determination at the highest sensitivity utilizing the developed sensor. As observed in Fig. 4 A, FLU shows absorption maxima around 300 nm. Thus, it was convenient to get a benefit from the excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior of the N,S-CQDs and modulating the λex for the probe’s best analytical performance in FLU analysis. Thus, λex/λem of 310/398 nm were selected to permit good sensitivity, spectral shape, as well as high fluorescence of the nanoprobe. Fig. S2 showed that the quenching comes to effect quantitatively with the increase in FLU concentration (1.0–30.0 µg mL−1). There are a variety of possible mechanisms for quenching, including the inner filter effect (IFE), static, and dynamic quenching. The IEF would happen if there was a spectral overlap between the UV-absorption band of the quencher and the excitation and/or emission wavelengths of the fluorophore. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the UV–Vis spectrum of FLU exhibits a characteristic absorption peak at 300 nm, which overlaps with λex of the as-obtained N,S-CQDs, implying the potential of primary IFE [57] that is significantly magnified by excitation at 310 nm. By calculating the corrected F in Eq. (2) [57] for further confirmation of the mechanism:
(2)
F
corrected
=
F
observed
× a n t i l o g
(
A
ex
+
A
em
2
)
where Fcorrected refers to the corrected FI upon removing IFE, Fobserved denotes the observed FI. The FLU absorbance is represented by (A) at λex and λem of the fluorescent N,S-CQDs.

After that, the fluorescence suppression efficiency (%E) was computed for both Fobserved and Fcorrected in accordance to the provided Eq. (3) [57]:
(3)
% E =
1 -
F /
F °
× 100
A relationship between %E and FLU concentrations (µg mL−1) was figured out (Fig. S3 A), indicating that IFE has a major contribution in the fluorescence quenching. Approximately, 40 % of %E is lost by neglecting IFE, demonstrating its role in the fluorescence quenching. However, another mechanism still responsible for 60 % of the fluorescence quenching.

Thus, Stern − Volmer equation was applied to elucidate the prospective quenching mechanism as follows:
(4)
F °
/ F = 1 +
K
sv
[ F L U ]
where, Ksv denotes the constant for the Stern Volmer quenching and it is calculated from the slope of (F○/F) versus [FLU].

Static quenching results from ground state non-luminescent complex formation, while collision-based dynamic quenching is caused by the contact of the fluorophore in its excited state with the quencher (FLU), leading to a non-radiative loss of energy [58]. So, in static quenching as the temperature increases, the formed ground state complex is cleaved, leading to a reduction in the Ksv value. While in case of dynamic quenching, elevation of the temperature enhances the molecular collision and increases the value of Ksv. Thus, Stern-Volmer plots were obtained by figuring the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F○/F) versus concentration of FLU [FLU] at three different temperatures. The results of Fig. S3 B and Table S1 demonstrated that Ksv decreased with raising temperature, denoting a static quenching mechanism. As complex formation strength tends to be inversely correlated with temperature, smaller static quenching occurs at higher temperatures. Fig. 2 B illustrates the potential mechanism for the non-fluorescent complex formation between the N,S-CQDs and FLU. Firstly, there is a charge transfer upon contact of CQDs with FLU, probably between the nitro group of FLU and the amino group of the CQDs [59,60]. Furthermore, a π–π stacking interaction involving aromatic moieties from the CQDs core and the benzene ring of FLU is expected. Besides, the nitrogen atom of CQDs makes weak Van der Waals interactions with the oxygen and fluorine of the analyte [61]. Additionally, the multiple function groups of the CQDs (OH–, COOH, and NH2) and the nitro and amide groups of FLU allowed a strong hydrogen bonding [59,61]. These likely modes of interaction between the synthesized CQDs and FLU explained static quenching as the major player in the fluorescence quenching process. Thus, IFE and static quenching contribute to the fluorescence quenching of N,S-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the experimental conditions for FLU determination
ce:section.ce:para: N,S-CQDs volume, pH, and incubation time were among the experimental parameters that were investigated and optimized since they may have an impact on the performance and sensitivity of the designed sensor. Firstly, the pH effect on F○/F was examined over the range of 2–12 utilizing BRB. Buffer had a little effect on the quenching efficiency (Fig. S4 A), so the study was completed without any buffer, and this adds the advantage of simplicity to the developed procedure.

The influence of the N,S-CQDs solution volume was tested in the 50–200 µL range to achieve the maximum F○/F value. The addition of 170 µL of that solution produced the maximum F○/F value; hence, it was chosen for the further experiment (Fig. S4 B).

The impact of the incubation period was investigated after mixing FLU and N,S-CQDs at different periods of time. The reaction occurred immediately, and F○/F values remained stable for more than one hour (Fig. S4 C).

The designed experiment was validated to agree with ICH recommendations [62]. The calibration plot was generated by plotting Ln (F○/F) against FLU concentration (µg mL−1). The approach was linear in the range of concentrations from 1.0 to 30.0 µg mL−1 with excellent linear relationship (correlation coefficient (r) = 0.9999). The linear regression equation was Ln (F○/F) = − 0.014 + 0.035C, where C represents FLU concentration (µg mL−1). In this study, the calculated regression data were listed in Table S2 . LOQ and LOD were computed ( Table S2
), referring to the subsequent ICH equations [62]:

LOD = 3.3
S a
/ b
and LOQ = 10 .
S a
/ b
where Sa is standard deviation of the calibration curve intercept, b denotes the regression line slope.

Besides, the accuracy was verified by determining the percentage recovery for the triplicate analysis of the FLU over the linearity range. The results obtained (Table 1 ) were compared statistically with those of the official British Pharmacopoeia method [3] using t test and F test [63]. The official method [3] is based on spectrophotometric measurement at 295 nm utilizing methanol as the diluting solvent. The results affirmed no statistically significant difference between the two procedures. The precision of the designed approach was investigated by assessing the intraday and interday precision. Regarding intraday precision, three different concentrations within the specified linear range were estimated three times on one day, while for interday precision, the same concentrations were analyzed on three different days. As illustrated in Table S3 , the small values of %RSD (≤1.72) indicate that the designed approach is precise.

Method’s robustness was evaluated by a deliberate minor change in the N,S-CQDs volume (170 ± 10 µL). As shown in Table S4 , this minor change did not have a significant influence on the FI quenching by FLU since the percentage recoveries were high and the %RSD values did not exceed 0.5.

Selectivity is among the most essential features of a sensor, which is the sensor’s response to the analyte of interest over other ions and molecules that may be present. The selectivity was studied by investigating the effect of possible interfering excipients such as sucrose, lactose, glucose, citric acid, ascorbic acid, hydroxypropyl methylcellulose, starch, Na2SO4, NaCl, KCl, MgCl2, and CaCl2 on the FI of the probe. Under optimal conditions, varied metals such as Ba2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Zn2+, and Ni2+ were introduced to the N,S-CQDs system, which had negligible influence on the probe fluorescence. Percentage of FI change was estimated by applying the equation:
(5)
% F =
(
F 2
-
F 1
)
/
F 1
× 100
F1 and F2 are FI of CQDs solution without and with interfering component, respectively. Thus, the probe can effectively be applied for FLU analysis in different matrices without significant interference. Commonly co-administered drugs with FLU, such as prednisolone, dexamethasone, spiramycin, paracetamol, and aceclofenac also did not interfere with FLU determination, as revealed from Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method application for real sample assay

ce:section.ce:para: The high selectivity obtained for FLU by this probe confirmed the excellent potential of the designed sensor to analyze FLU in its tablets. Table 1 lists the results obtained from applying N,S-CQDs in pharmaceutical tablets. These results were compared with those of the official HPLC method [3]. There is no discernible difference between the two methods based on the statistical analysis using the t-test and F-test [63] (Table 1). The high recovery and small SD resulted from the designed method (99.59 ± 1.28), also indicate the method’s ability for reliable analysis of FLU in tablet formulations without any interference from the excipients present in these formulations. Hence, the designed method is promising for application in quality control labs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FLU analysis in tap and river water
ce:section.ce:para: FLU has been found as a pharmaceutical emergent contaminant in environmental water [64–66]. Therefore, it is expected that rising FLU production and usage in the upcoming years will lead to a rise in water resource pollution and thereby affect the ecosystem. FLU exposure has been shown to cause severe hepatotoxicity by targeting hepatic mitochondria and generating oxidative stress, which alters mitochondrial protein and DNA and increases hepatocellular apoptosis with smaller necrotic areas [67]. Besides, it could lead to rectal bleeding, decreased sexual interest, and larger male breast growth [68]. Consequently, it has become mandatory to analyze FLU in water bodies.

The proposed method has been applied to screen tap water samples obtained from our laboratory as well as water sample from the Nile River to detect traces of FLU. The obtained results indicated that the concentrations of FLU in tap and river water were less than the LOD of the method. Upon spiking tap and river water with the drug, the percentage recovery values for the analysis were in the range of 97.41–102.82 % and the %RSD did not exceed 1.76 (Table 1), demonstrating the reliability and convenience of the suggested method for monitoring FLU in environmental samples without significant matrix effect.

The ranking of analytical techniques based on how closely they conform to the green analytical chemistry principles has altered due to the worldwide trend towards more sustainable analytical methods [69]. The metrics related to green chemistry comprise all the different parts of a chemical process that are in line with the core ideas of green chemistry. The green character of the studied approach was assessed using two complementary tools. The first is ComplexGAPI [36], which assesses the whole approach’s factors, such as sample preparation, transport, reagents and solvents, and instruments. It also comprises the procedures and actions taken prior to applying the overall analytical methodology. ComplexGAPI metric is represented by five pentagrams with an additional hexagonal field. This field reflects how “green” pre-analysis procedures are. The green color predominates in the pictogram, as presented in Fig. 5
A.

The second tool is AGREE [37], automated software that offers a simple and flexible assessment. It offers a clock-like circle symbol with 12 numbers that each represents a principle of the 12 green analytical chemistry with a central numerical scale ranging from 0 to 1. This symbol and scale have a color scale ranging from dark green to dark red, reflecting low and high environmental hazards, respectively. The suggested spectrofluorimetric approach’s AGREE score is 0.78, with green color predominating in the symbol (Fig. 5 B), proving its greenness.

Researchers might go deeper into the investigation, concentrating on the scope of sustainability issues. This requires a thorough examination of areas that need to be improved or mitigated, as well as assessing the method's practicality. The recently created White Analytical Chemistry concept is employed to assess the analytical method's quality in terms of instrument handling proficiency, cost, time, and validation efficiency [70]. The RGB12 algorithm evaluates the whiteness and has three colors that reflect three principles (red, green, and blue reflecting analytical, greenness principles, and practicality, respectively). The whiteness% is the total of the three regions’ scores [71]. Considering the red region, the proposed spectrofluorimetric method has a high score of 93.8. According to the green region, the score was great (96.3), and the method scored 90.8 regarding the blue region. So, the proposed method was white, fitted to the intended application, with a score of 93.6, as presented in Fig. 5 C.

Blue Applicability Grade Index (BAGI), a recently developed gadget, offers a quantitative mechanism for assessing the “blueness” or applicability of analytical techniques. It offered supplementary insights to better grasp the economic and practical criteria. Two sets of results are produced by this tool: a numerical score at the center of the asteroid pictogram and a graphical depiction of the data [38]. The geometric mean of the scores for ten parameters in the analytical process is calculated to create the composite BAGI score. More application, functionality, and suitability for the intended use are indicated by a higher BAGI score. The pictogram has different blue color grades (dark blue, blue, and light blue reflect high to low compliance, with white color reflecting non-compliance at all) [38]. Our approach achieves a BAGI score of 72.5. The results of the BAGI evaluation confirm that our method has a great deal to offer in terms of general functioning, hazard minimization, and time and cost savings, as displayed in Fig. 5 D.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison between the proposed method and the published spectrophotometric and spectrofluorimetric ones for the tested analyte
ce:section.ce:para: As tabulated in Table S5 , the developed sensor offers a lower quantitation limit compared to photometric methods and a wider linearity range than the other spectrofluorimetric ones. Besides, this method is rapid, simpler, easier to handle, and cost-effective compared to published methods. The reported spectrofluorimetric procedure [21] used Zn dust with concentrated HCl and needed time for neutralization with NaHCO3. The other reported spectrofluorimetry [22] is tedious and time consuming as it requires mixing pepsin with CuSO4 for 5 min and neutralization with NaOH, then further mixing with hydrazine for 2.0 h at 50 ○C with the release of heat to the environment. Hydrazine is toxic and potentially carcinogenic, and can cause acute and chronic damage to many organs [72].

In this study, sweet yellow pepper was explored as a feasible alternative input carbon and nitrogen source for sustainable CQDs production. Due to the cheapness and availability of the raw materials, the simplicity and mildness of the synthetic conditions, as well as the selectivity study for FLU, our developed method is ideally applied in different real matrices. For the first time, a natural fabricated sensor was employed for the determination of FLU in tap water and river water with high %recovery, which in turn offers help to analysts caring about environmental pollution with FLU. It is superior to the documented methods in completing the separation process in much less time, which in turn enhances sample throughput, saves cost, and produces less waste and toxic reagents consumption. The cost-efficiency of the studied method encourages its widespread use in low-income laboratories.

The studied microwave-assisted synthesis of plant-based CQDs proved its predominance thanks to its easy application, reliance on readily available domestic microwave, enhanced greenness, whiteness, and blueness of the method, less energy consumption, and acceptable robustness. It is also the first time to apply greenness, whiteness, and blueness assessments to assure effective practicability.

The studied method is 30–140 times quicker than the documented procedures. Our methodology is a mix-and-read method because there is no dangerous chemical involved during the entire process, and the reaction between FLU and N,S-CQDs is immediate. In contrast, the documented synthesis methods [21,22] require high temperatures and very long times.

This method [22] was not fully validated regarding the ICH protocol. Regarding linearity, r values were 0.9934 [21] and 0.9956 [22] in the two previously spectrofluorimetric procedures, compared to r of 0.9999 achieved in our method adhering to ICH requirements [62] (ICH recommends r higher than 0.998). Additionally, the reported pepsin-CuCQDs probe [22] was mixed with FLU for 10 min prior to analysis and showed low FI in the pH range 2–8. Its stability lasted just 7 days, while the stability of our probe lasted up to 6 months. On the other hand, the studied novel method depends on microwave radiation for the synthesis of N,S-CQDs from sweet yellow pepper juice and thiourea, which are easily available materials without heating at high temperatures. Microwave synthesis is advantageous due to the greener synthesis process as well as the carbon supply, homogenous heating, quick kinetics, and excellent phase purity. Furthermore, controlling particle size distribution is another advantage of microwave synthesis, as shorter reaction times tend to have narrower particle size distribution [73]. The method employed is the first spectrofluorimetric one for the quantification of FLU in tap and river water.

In this work, a simple, green, white, blue, economical, quick, and selective spectrofluorimetric approach was established for the determination of FLU in pharmaceutical tablets, tap water, and river water. A one-pot microwave-assisted synthesis of fluorescent N,S-CQDs were synthesized from sweet yellow pepper and thiourea and characterized by various microscopical and optical characterization techniques. FLU can turn off the fluorescence of N,S-CQDs via IFE and static quenching. The probe was used for the first time for the determination of FLU in tap and river water with excellent percentage recoveries (99.49 ± 1.75 and 101.21 ± 1.67, respectively) and without interference. As well, it was applied for analysis of dosage form containing FLU with percentage recovery of 99.59 ± 1.28. The synthesized probe is characterized by high stability for several months, easily available eco-friendly starting materials, reproducibility of synthesis procedure in various seasons and regions, excellent water solubility, and remarkable selectivity. Furthermore, the developed probe interestingly showed acceptable cytotoxicity, permitting its use in environmental water containing living organisms. Four greenness assessment tools namely, GAPI, AGREE, RGB12, and the most recent BAGI were employed to evaluate the greenness, whiteness, and blueness of the present study, which displayed sustainability, analytical efficiency, and cost-effectiveness, The suggested approach does not require a high level of technical expertise, complicated sample preparation stages, hazardous organic solvents, or derivatizing agents. So, this sustainable method can be efficiently implemented in routine quality control laboratories and environmental monitoring.
